content: Mendelian randomization offers clearer insights than epidemiology or randomized trials to date.
Biddinger et al. found that all levels of alcohol exposure were associated with increased risk of cardiovascular diseases.
Each standard deviation increase in genetically predicted alcohol consumption was associated with a 30% higher risk of hypertension (OR=1.3; 95% CI: 1.2-1.4; P<0.001) over the ten-year follow-up.
Even among the light drinking group, every one-drink per day increase in alcohol consumption was found to significantly raise risk of hypertension by 30% (OR=1.3; 95%CI: 1.1-1.5; P=0.003).
A person who drinks, say, three glasses of wine per day (21 drinks per week) can reduce their CAD risk by over two-thirds just by shifting to two glasses per day (14 drinks per week).
